God.text: Heb. Elohim, meaning the Mighty One. The name is plural, but the verb is singular. This is a seed of the Trinity (Matt. 28:19). God is one (Isa. 45:5; 1 Cor. 8:4), but He is also three — the Father, the Son, and the Spirit.

Spirit.text: Heb. ruach, variously translated spirit, wind, breath. The Spirit of God, as the Spirit of life (Rom. 8:2), comes to brood over death in order to generate life.

created.text: Heb. bara. To bring something into existence out of nothing. Differs from 'made' or 'formed' (Gen. 2:4, 7). God's creation is to glorify the Son (Col. 1:15-19) and manifest God (Rom. 1:20).

heavens.text: In the process of God’s creation, the heavens and the angels therein were created first (Job 38:4-7).

life.text: The central theme of the Bible. Gr. Zoe. In the New Testament, this refers to the eternal, divine life of God (John 1:4; 11:25), represented by the tree of life in (Gen. 2:9). God's intention is to dispense this life into man.

grace.text: Grace is God in Christ as our enjoyment. It is not just an unmerited favor; it is the Triune God processed and dispensed into us for our support and sufficiency (John 1:17; Gal. 6:18; 2 Cor. 12:9).

truth.text: Gr. aletheia. In the Recovery Version, truth denotes the reality of the divine things. It is not just doctrine, but the Triune God and His economy as the absolute reality (John 14:6; 17:17).

Spirit of God.text: The Spirit of God is the reaching out of the Triune God to man. From the 'brooding' in (Gen. 1:2) to the 'life-giving Spirit' in (1 Cor. 15:45), the Spirit is the application of God to His creation.

Trinity.text: The Triune God—Father, Son, and Spirit. The Father is the source, the Son is the expression, and the Spirit is the transmission. They are distinct but not separate (Matt. 28:19; 2 Cor. 13:14).

economy.text: Gr. oikonomia. Meaning 'house law' or 'household management.' It refers to God's plan to distribute Himself into His chosen people to produce the Church, the Body of Christ (Eph. 1:10; 3:9; 1 Tim. 1:4).

flesh.text: Referring to the fallen human nature (Rom. 7:18). In the Bible, flesh is the outward part of man corrupted by sin, which is in enmity with the Spirit (Gal. 5:17).

Kingdom.text: The Kingdom of God is the sphere where God exercises His authority to fulfill His purpose. It is also the spreading of Christ as life into His believers (Mark 4:26-29; Rom. 14:17).

man.text: Created in God's image and likeness (Gen. 1:26) to be a vessel to contain God (Rom. 9:23-24). Man is a three-part being: spirit, soul, and body (1 Thes. 5:23).

image.text: The outward expression of God. Christ is the image of the invisible God (Col. 1:15). Man was created in this image to express Christ.

likeness.text: The inward form or fashion of God. Man was made in God's likeness so that he might look like God and eventually be one with God in life and nature.

rest.text: God rested on the seventh day because He had obtained a man to express Him and exercise His authority (Gen. 2:2). Our rest is found in Christ (Matt. 11:28).

light.text: The first thing God brought in to overcome the darkness (Gen. 1:3). Christ is the true light that shines into the darkness of man (John 1:5, 9).

seed.text: A crucial promise. First mentioned as the 'seed of the woman' (Gen. 3:15) to bruise the serpent's head, which is fulfilled in Christ (Gal. 4:4; Heb. 2:14).

blessing.text: To be blessed by God is to have God Himself as our portion. The ultimate blessing is the processed and dispensed Triune God (Gen. 1:28; Gal. 3:14).

beginning.text: Refers to the beginning of time. Time started with creation (Gen. 1:1) and continues until the final judgment. Time serves the carrying out of God's eternal purpose.

became.text: Indicates a change of condition. Verse 1 records God's original creation (Gen. 1:1), while verse 2 shows the earth in a judged condition before the six days of restoration (Gen. 1:2).

waste and emptiness.text: This condition points to the result of divine judgment. In the Old Testament, this expression is associated with desolation under God's judgment (Gen. 1:2).

darkness.text: Darkness here signifies the effect of judgment and death upon the earth before God's restoring work began (Gen. 1:2-3).

deep.text: The deep water signifies death. The earth being covered by the deep shows that it was under death as a result of God's judgment on the preadamic universe (Gen. 1:2).

brooding.text: The Spirit's brooding is like a bird hovering over its young to generate life. It shows the Spirit of life coming over death in order to bring forth life for God's purpose (Gen. 1:2; Rom. 8:2).

Day.text: Day is distinguished from night by the separation of light from darkness (Gen. 1:4-5). Spiritually, this points to discernment and the division between what belongs to God and what does not.

expanse.text: The expanse refers to the atmosphere or the air between the waters (Gen. 1:6-8). Spiritually, it signifies a separating work that divides the heavenly things from the earthly things.

gathered together.text: The gathering of the waters prepares the way for dry land to appear (Gen. 1:9). This is part of God's ordering work so that life may be produced.

dry land.text: The appearing of the dry land is related to the generation of life (Gen. 1:9-12). Spiritually, land signifies a standing place out of death where life can be produced.

Earth.text: The earth becomes the sphere where life is generated and where God's purpose in creation is carried out, especially through man (Gen. 1:1, 11-12, 26-28).

Seas.text: The seas are the gathered waters (Gen. 1:10). In biblical symbolism, the sea often points to death, instability, and separation from the solid ground for life.

light-bearers.text: The light-bearers were appointed to shine upon the earth and to govern times and seasons (Gen. 1:14-18). They serve God's arrangement in creation.

signs.text: Signs indicate markers or indicators in the heavens that help distinguish appointed times and divine ordering within creation (Gen. 1:14).

seasons.text: Seasons refer to appointed times. They show that God's creation includes order, regulation, and times set according to His arrangement (Gen. 1:14).

great sea creatures.text: These are the large creatures of the waters created by God according to their kinds, showing the richness and variety of life in creation (Gen. 1:21).

living animals.text: Living animals are creatures with soul-life. Their creation shows the progression of life in Genesis 1 leading toward the creation of man (Gen. 1:20-21, 24, 30).

swarms.text: The swarming of the waters indicates abundance and multiplication in the created life under God's blessing (Gen. 1:20-22).

cattle.text: Cattle are one category of land animals created according to their kind, showing God's ordered and purposeful creation (Gen. 1:24-25).

creeping things.text: Creeping things are another category of earthly creatures created according to their kind, demonstrating the completeness of God's creation (Gen. 1:24-25).

Us.text: This is a seed of the Divine Trinity. God is one, yet in creation He speaks in a plural way, indicating the Father, the Son, and the Spirit (Gen. 1:26; Matt. 28:19).

dominion.text: Man was created not only to express God but also to represent Him with authority. Dominion points to man's commission to rule for God over the earth (Gen. 1:26, 28).

subdue.text: To subdue the earth means to bring it under God's rule through man. It implies dealing with rebellion and recovering the earth for God's purpose (Gen. 1:28).

living soul.text: A living soul refers to the soulish life in the animal creation (Gen. 1:20, 21, 24, 30). This distinguishes animal life from the higher purpose of man as a vessel to contain God.

very good.text: This indicates God's full satisfaction with His restoring and creating work. Everything was complete, ordered, and suitable for His purpose (Gen. 1:31).

Shepherd.text: Christ is the Good Shepherd who cares for our souls (Psa. 23:1) and laid down His life for the sheep (John 10:11).
